Pulmonary infection may result, as may a build-up of carbon dioxide, due to the hypoventilation which impairs to the level of the alveoli. Patients with hypothyroidism may often be overweight or obese, which can contribute to the further respiratory complication of sleep apnea, worsening the respiratory status. Patients who die as a result of myxedema coma most often do so as a result of respiratory failure. Glomerular filtration rate is decreased in myxedema coma, as is renal blood flow. Patients with myxedema coma most often experience hypothermia, not hyperthermia (hyperpyrexia). The hyperthermia of thyroid storm should not be treated with salicylates. Cardiovascular complications include low cardiac output, decreased heart rate, and decreased stroke volume.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient suffering from decompression sickness. He is confused, has visual disturbances, and has had seizures prior to your arrival. What type of decompression sickness is the patient experiencing?
rationale: Type 2 decompression sickness is defined by neurological changes. Type 1 is associated with bone and joint pain. While there are several types of decompression sickness, the aeromedical provider usually is only concerned with type 1 and type 2.

stem: Which of the following statements about the use and functioning of an intraaortic balloon pump (IABP) during medical air transport is most accurate?
options: During ascent to altitude, the IABP needs to be manually purged with helium
Refilling of the balloon with helium occurs automatically every 2 hours during medical air transport
During medical air transport, the balloon requires manual filling by the medical crew due to changes in balloon volume due to altitude changes
Use of the IABP during medical air transport requires that triggers and timing be controlled manually by the medical crew
answer: Refilling of the balloon with helium occurs automatically every 2 hours during medical air transport
rationale: Intraaortic balloon pumps (IABPs) continue to be frequently encountered by the medical crews of medical air transport programs as they continue to transport critically ill patients to medical centers which provide the highest level of cardiac care. The IABP may be placed in automatic mode or manual mode during air transport. If it is placed in manual mode, the medical crew will be required to select and control all the triggers and timing of triggers during flight, as well as manually fill the balloon with helium to adjust for the atmospheric pressure changes which affect the gas. During manual control of balloon filling, the team will need to fill the balloon every 2,000 feet during ascent and every 1,000 feet during descent. The most modern versions of IABPs typically have an automatic balloon refill setting, even when the pump is placed in manual mode, and the balloon is refilled every 2 hours, as well as more frequently based on changes noted by volume sensors.

stem: The medical air transport team receives a call from a referring hospital, requesting air transport of a patient who sustained a primary spontaneous pneumothorax. Based on knowledge of the population most at risk for a primary spontaneous pneumothorax, which of the following patients should the transport team expect to receive?
options: A 17-year-old white male weighing 61.3 kg and measuring 74 inches tall
A 38-year-old Asian male weighing 65 kg and measuring 69 inches tall
A 14-year-old black female weighing 70.4 kg and measuring 67 inches tall
A 49-year-old Hispanic female weighing 61.3 kg and measuring 61 inches tall
answer: A 17-year-old white male weighing 61.3 kg and measuring 74 inches tall
rationale: Spontaneous pneumothorax may occur as a primary disorder without any precipitating factors, or as a secondary response to an underlying pathology such as asthma, emphysema, tuberculosis, and any of the interstitial lung diseases. Pneumothorax may also occur as a result of trauma to the chest (this is not considered a spontaneous pneumothorax). In spontaneous pneumothorax, the lung collapses, and air accumulates in the pleural space, further limiting the lung's ability to perform work of breathing. Individuals most at risk for primary spontaneous pneumothorax are tall, thin teenage males; genetics, a history of smoking or previous pneumothorax, and lung disease are other risk factors for development of spontaneous pneumothorax.

stem: A patient who is in moderate congestive heart failure is being transported via medical air transport. The patient is receiving non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NPPV) using CPAP, and has also received two doses of nitroglycerin. The patient is secured in supine position for takeoff of the aircraft and, shortly after takeoff, experiences cardiac arrest. Typically, during cardiac arrest which takes place during air transport, how many members of the air transport crew are able to participate in the resuscitation using advanced life support?
options: All the crew members, minus the pilot
rationale: All the members of the medical air transport crew are expected to obtain and maintain their certification both in basic life support (BLS) skills and advanced cardiac life support (ACLS) skills. While, due to the closing of hospitals in some rural areas and staffing shortages in other areas, the medical air transport crews may now be transporting fewer critically ill patients than previously and be seeing an increase in the transport of more stable patients, the risk of cardiac arrest during air transport remains high. Providing ACLS in the cramped space of an aircraft poses many challenges, among them, a limit to the number of crew members who can actively participate in the provision of ACLS at any one time (2). The medical crew members must be intimately familiar with their role expectations during cardiac arrest to ensure effective delivery of ACLS.

stem: While transporting a patient who has an Impella Ventricular Support System device in place, the "suction event" alarm sounds. What step(s) should the medical transport crew take in resolving the issue which caused the sounding of the alarm?
options: Decrease the RPMs and administer intravenous (IV) fluid
Begin providing flow through use of the manual hand crank
Apply pressure to the catheter insertion site
answer: Decrease the RPMs and administer intravenous (IV) fluid
rationale: Impella Ventricular Support Systems are catheter-based devices which are used to treat the cardiac failure associated with cardiogenic shock which persists after cardiac surgery or myocardial infarction (MI). These ventricular assist devices (VADs) may be used to support failure of either the left or right ventricle, and are typically inserted via the femoral arteries, with right ventricular failure being treated by placement of the catheter in the pulmonary artery, and left ventricular failure being treated by placement of the catheter directly into the left ventricle after being advanced through the ascending aorta. The device works to support ventricular function by use of an impeller which, through the use of centrifugal force and an increasing number of RPMs, pumps blood. The RPM speed is referred to as "P levels," which range from 10,000 RPM (the lowest and slowest speed) to 33,000 RPM. The P level should not be adjusted by the medical air transport crew, except in the sounding of a "suction event" alarm, or if the patient requires cardiopulmonary resuscitation. Suction events occur as a result of a decrease in the filling of the pump (a decrease in preload) and the pump is offloading blood at the same rate. This results in a portion of the ventricular wall being sucked over the inlet cannula of the pump, which then triggers the pump to decrease the RPMs in an attempt to release the suction. The medical transport crew should decrease the P level accordingly to facilitate this release of suction.

stem: After administration of first-line medication treatment for acute coronary syndrome (ACS), all of the following medications may be used as next steps in treatment of ACS, except:
rationale: Acute coronary syndrome (ACS), which encompasses three acute cardiac events (unstable angina, non-ST elevation myocardial infarction \[NSTEMI\], and acute ST elevation myocardial infarction \[STEMI\]), results from atherosclerotic plaque rupture within the coronary vessels, and may ultimately lead to the patient's death from sudden cardiac death. Patients who are believed to be experiencing an ACS event should be rapidly evaluated and provided with initial treatment, which includes the administration of oxygen, nitroglycerin, aspirin, and morphine (some programs do not use morphine in treatment of the chest pain associated with ACS, and simply rely on nitroglycerin for this). Once initial treatment has been administered, additional medications may be used to continue treatment of ACS. These include the administration of beta blocker medications, such as metoprolol, to decrease myocardial oxygen consumption; heparin, to reduce the further formation of thromboses (heparin does not dissolve already established clots); and a glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor, such as abciximab, which inhibits platelet aggregation.

stem: Which answer explains why using nitroglycerin in patients suffering from an inferior MI should be used with caution or altogether avoided?
options: Nitroglycerin is a potent vasodilator and causes a decrease in heart rate, lowering blood pressure

Nitroglycerin is a potent vasodilator and, as such, causes pooling in the venous system with reduced return back to the heart. This cause less preload in the right ventricle and reduces contractility to an already damaged right side.
There are no risks involved with using nitroglycerin in patients with inferior MI
Nitroglycerin is a potent vasoconstrictor and will cause too much afterload on the right ventricle causing right sided heart failure
answer: Nitroglycerin is a potent vasodilator and, as such, causes pooling in the venous system with reduced return back to the heart. This cause less preload in the right ventricle and reduces contractility to an already damaged right side.
rationale: Nitroglycerin is a potent vasodilator and, as such, causes pooling in the venous system with reduced return back to the heart. This causes less preload in the right ventricle and reduces the contractility of an already damaged right side. This question requires the knowledge of the Frank-Starling Law and that the myocardium will contract more forcefully when there is more blood in the ventricle to stretch the muscle. Nitro will cause venous pooling, thus reducing blood return to the right side, which is already damaged in an inferior MI. This will, in turn, cause reduced stroke volume and reduced cardiac output to the pulmonary circulation and thus, to the left side of the heart, resulting in systemic hypotension.

stem: Which of the following answers describes a bundle branch block?
rationale: The normal range for QRS is 0.08 to 0.12 seconds. QRS segments that are greater than 0.12 seconds are considered bundle branch blocks. A PR segment of greater than 0.20 seconds is a first degree AV block. A PR interval that is less than 0.20 seconds is normal.

stem: You are the team leader transporting a pulseless, three-year-old male patient in Torsades de Pointes. High quality CPR is in progress. His baseline-corrected QT interval on a 12-lead ECG is prolonged, he has received one dose of epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg, but he remains in Torsades de Pointes. What is the best next step?
rationale: Immediate defibrillation is the treatment of choice when Torsades is sustained or degenerates to VF. However, termination of Torsades by shock does not prevent its recurrence, which requires additional measures. In small case series, IV magnesium has been effective in suppressing and preventing recurrences of Torsades. Magnesium is believed to suppress early after-depolarizations, which are fluctuations in the myocardial action potential that can trigger the salvos of VT seen in Torsades. Magnesium is the initial treatment for Torsades de Pointes and dysrhythmias secondary to hypomagnesemia, cardiac arrest from QTc prolongation, or cardiac glycoside toxicity. Pacing is continued until the cause of QT prolongation (drug toxicity, electrolyte abnormality) is resolved.

stem: What is the worst timing error for an IABP (Intraaortic balloon pump)?
rationale: Late deflation in the IABP means that the balloon inflated during systole, causing an increased workload and increased afterload. While the other options are not ideal, they are not as harmful to the patient as late deflation.

stem: A medical air transport crew is en route to pick up a patient who has been diagnosed with syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH) for transport. Which of the following laboratory findings is consistent with this diagnosis?
rationale: SIADH occurs when the pituitary gland releases excessive amounts of ADH, which originates in the hypothalamus and is stored in the pituitary gland. Increased production of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) causes decreased urinary output and fluid overload, resulting in hyponatremia. Fluid overload causes hypokalemia (not hyperkalemia) and low osmolality (\<275 mOsm/L). Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) is normal or decreased.

stem: The medical air transport team is providing care for a patient with diabetes mellitus who is lethargic with slurred speech and emesis. These assessment findings are consistent with which of the following?
rationale: Increased ICP manifests as speech disturbances, visual changes, lethargy, or motor weakness imitating stroke. A patient with increased ICP may also have emesis. Increased ICP is a potential complication of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), which can occur in both diagnosed and undiagnosed diabetes mellitus. The other choices may be possible causes of lethargy, slurred speech, and emesis if diabetes mellitus is absent.

stem: For which of the following group designations of pulmonary hypertension (PH) would the treatment modalities include the use of calcium channel blockers and endothelin receptor antagonists?
rationale: Pulmonary hypertension (PH) is a disorder of elevated blood pressure within the pulmonary arteries and right ventricle, classified by the WHO into 5 categories. Group 1 PH is characterized by the constriction of vessels to the lungs, causing stress to the right ventricle, ultimately leading to right-sided heart failure and compromise of left ventricular function. Group 1 PH may be caused by heredity, induced by drugs or toxin exposures, or connective tissue disorders. Group 1 PH is treated by the use of calcium channel blockers, prostanoids (lipid mediators), phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, and endothelin receptor antagonists. Group 2 PH is characterized by valvular disease and left ventricle involvement; treatment is aimed at managing preload, afterload, and cardiac contractility. Group 3 PH is characterized by chronic lung disease and hypoxia; it is managed by treating the cause of the hypoxia. Group 4 PH is characterized by chronic thromboembolism and is treated with blood thinners. Group 5 PH is multifactorial, caused by hematologic, systemic, and metabolic disorders; treatment is aimed at correcting the underlying disorder.

stem: Which medication is given for Torsades de Pointes?
rationale: Torsades de Pointes is controlled by the use of magnesium. Amiodarone is used for V-tach and V-fib, as is lidocaine. Adenosine is given for supraventricular tachycardia.

stem: According to current ACLS cardiac pharmacology guidelines, which of the following drugs should be administered as a first-line agent to treat sinus bradycardia?
rationale: The 2015 ACLS guidelines for the pharmacologic treatment of acute sinus bradycardia recommends the intravenous (IV) administration of atropine at a dose of 0.5 mg, which can be repeated every 3 to 5 minutes, to a maximum dosage of 3 mg. If atropine is not effective at re-establishing a life-sustaining rhythm, the medical air crew should prepare to apply transcutaneous pacing (TCP) as a temporary measure, and will need to medicate the patient appropriately for this external measure due to the discomfort it typically causes. Dopamine may be used as a second-line treatment for sinus bradycardia, as may epinephrine. Adenosine is used to treat narrow complex SVT. Magnesium sulfate is used to treat torsades de pointes.

stem: During transport of a patient, symptomatic bradycardia is refractory to atropine. Per ACLS protocols, what is the next best treatment?
rationale: Bradycardia may be difficult to resolve until the underlying cause is treated, making evaluation of underlying cause imperative, simultaneous with emergent treatments for stabilization. Atropine has been shown to be effective for the treatment of symptomatic bradycardia in both observational studies and in one limited RCT. If atropine is ineffective, either alternative agents to increase heart rate and blood pressure or transcutaneous pacing are reasonable next steps. For medical management of a peri-arrest patient, epinephrine has gained popularity, including IV infusion and utilization of "push-dose" administration for acute bradycardia and hypotension. Recommended dose of epinephrine IV infusion is 2-10 mcg/min. All other options are treatment for tachycardia and/or ventricular arrhythmias, and are inappropriate for bradycardia.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient with the following lab values: ALT: 60 U/L AST: 50 U/L Albumin 3.0 g/dL Bilirubin 1.7 mg/dL Increased serum ammonia A BUN to Creatinine ratio greater than 30:1 What is the underlying condition affecting this patient?
rationale: Increases in bilirubin, ALT, and AST are all signs of liver disease. Sepsis is a clinical syndrome of life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated response to infection (labs typically include low pH and elevated LDH, CRP and ESR). Acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (a.k.a. ARDS) is severe arterial hypoxemia that is refractory to supplemental oxygen (lab values would include low PaO2). Acute, severe bleeding can occur from anywhere in the GI tract; these patients may present with hypotension and signs of shock (lab values may include low hemoglobin/hematocrit).

stem: Your patient develops sudden onset narrow-complex tachycardia at a rate of 208 bpm, with BP 128/56, EtCO2 of 36 mmHg, and SpO2 of 97%. Two large-bore IVs have been placed, and a 12-lead ECG shows SVT without evidence of ischemia/infarction. Vagal maneuvers have no visible effect. What is the next best step?
rationale: The 2020 AHA guidelines for ACLS state that for a regular narrow-complex tachycardia, without evidence of hypotension, altered mental status, signs of shock, or chest pain/discomfort, the next treatment options are vagal maneuvers, adenosine, administration of a beta-blocker or calcium channel blocker, and the consideration of obtaining expert consultation.

stem: You are caring for a 60-year-old female who recently had an intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) placed. While in transit to another facility for higher level of care, the patient begins to complain of left arm pain and her left hand feels cold. As the CFRN, you assess both radial pulses to find the left radial pulse is greatly diminished as compared to the right. What is the most likely cause?
options: The IABP has migrated upward, occluding the left subclavian artery
The patient is experiencing another myocardial infarction
answer: The IABP has migrated upward, occluding the left subclavian artery
rationale: The correct position for the balloon to rest in is below the left subclavian artery and above the renal arteries in the descending aorta. Most likely, the balloon has migrated too far forward and is obstructing the left subclavian artery, causing reduced blood flow to the left arm. If the patient was having another myocardial infarction, there would be EKG changes and pulses would not differ between arms. Failure of the IABP to inflate would also not cause differences in pulses between arms. Aortic dissection would present with the patient complaining of severe, burning pain in the abdomen and back.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient that begins to experience SVT while in flight. Which medication should you administer?
rationale: Adenosine is used to treat SVT that does not respond to vagal maneuvers. Amiodarone is used to treat ventricular tachycardia, as is lidocaine. Atropine treats bradycardia and is an antidote for organophosphate poisonings.

stem: Which of the following is most appropriate for a patient in asystole?
rationale: Atropine and transcutaneous pacing are interventions for bradycardia; synchronized cardioversion is appropriate for patients with a cardiac arrhythmia. ACLS guidelines for the treatment of asystole is compressions and epinephrine.

stem: Which of the following sets of vital signs indicate adult sepsis?
rationale: Sepsis is a life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection. Clinical criteria for sepsis is related to suspected or documented infection and an acute increase of >2 SOFA points; a positive qSOFA (quick SOFA) criteria should also prompt consideration of possible infection in patients not previously recognized as infected. qSOFA criteria: Altered mental status (GCS score \<15); systolic blood pressure \<100 mmHg; respiratory rate >22 breaths per min.

stem: What is the recommended first dose of amiodarone for a patient with refractory ventricular fibrillation?
rationale: For cardiac arrest, amiodarone is used after the third shock for ventricular fibrillation (VF) and ventricular tachycardia (VT) that is unresponsive to shock delivery, CPR, and vasopressors. For tachycardia with a pulse, amiodarone may be considered, and expert consultation should be obtained prior to its use. Do not use amiodarone for individuals with polymorphic VT associated with a prolonged QT interval because this may worsen the patient's condition. Amiodarone is only used after defibrillation (or cardioversion) and epinephrine (first line medication) fail to convert VT/VF. The first dose of amiodarone for a patient with refractory ventricular fibrillation is 300 mg IV; if a second dose is given, it would be 150 mg IV. Beyond two doses, there is no evidence of benefit.

stem: What medication is given for acetaminophen overdoses?
rationale: Acetaminophen poisoning can cause gastroenteritis within hours and hepatotoxicity 1 to 3 days after ingestion. Severity of hepatotoxicity after a single acute overdose (>7.5 g in adults; >150 mg/kg in children) is predicted by serum acetaminophen levels. There are four generally accepted stages of acetaminophen toxicity. The first phase of APAP toxicity occurs within the first 24 hours and is largely asymptomatic, although the patient may experience a general malaise, some nausea/vomiting, or abdominal pain. The second phase occurs 24 to 72 hours post-ingestion, and any symptomology from phase 1 become less pronounced. The hepatic damage that occurs may result in some right upper quadrant pain, and blood chemistries will become abnormal (transaminitis, elevated INR, elevated bilirubin). Phase 3 occurs from 72 to 96 hours post-ingestion as hepatic failure continues (jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, AKI). Nausea and vomiting may reappear, and death is frequently preceded by anuria and coma. Phase 4 (4 days to 2 weeks post-ingestion): Survival or death. If the damage from Phase 3 is reversible, complete resolution will occur. For acetaminophen overdose, adequate GI decontamination consists of the early administration of activated charcoal orally or through a nasogastric tube within 90 minutes of ingestion. For overdose within 8 hours, treatment is with N-acetylcysteine (NAC) to prevent or minimize hepatotoxicity. Beyond 8 hours, treatment approach still utilizes NAC, but efficacy is greatly reduced. Glutathione and Cysteine were used experimentally as possible treatments but abandoned due to ineffectiveness. S-Adenosyl-l-methionine has been suggested as a treatment alternative to NSAIDs for reducing functional limitations associated with osteoarthritis, though it does not reduce pain.

stem: All of the following statements regarding toxic shock syndrome (TSS) are false, except:
options: small percentage of women develop antibodies to the organism responsible for causing TSS
Most cases of TSS are result from Group B streptococcus infection
Twenty-five percent of TSS cases occur in males
Twenty-five percent of TSS cases are associated with the use of long-absorbing tampons
answer: Twenty-five percent of TSS cases occur in males
rationale: Toxic shock syndrome (TSS) is an uncommonly occurring but potentially deadly complication of a bacterial infection caused by the presence of toxin-producing Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) or Streptococcus pyogenes (S. pyogenes). Mortality from TSS remains high at approximately 10% of individuals, with 50% of cases resulting from the use of long-absorbing tampons. S. aureus occurs naturally in the vagina in just under 10% of women, and most women develop antibodies to the organism. And while TSS is most often associated with women experiencing menstrual cycles, 25% of cases of TSS occur in males. Five percent of asymptomatic males have S. aureus present in urethral cultures. TSS may also occur with septic abortion, postpartum wound infections, chorioamnionitis, infected skin lesions, osteomyelitis, and periodontal abscess, among others.

stem: A patient with staphylococcus aureus pneumonia who is being prepared for medical air transport is currently being treated with clindamycin 600 mg IV administered every 8 hours, IV fluids, and is being oxygenated via a high-flow nasal cannula device. The patient has a history of alcoholism, is a type II diabetic, and recently had an influenza infection. Which of the following risk factors is the most likely cause of the patient's S. aureus pneumonia?
rationale: Pneumonia is defined as the inflammation of the lung parenchyma which may be caused by viral, bacterial, or fungal organisms, or it may be caused by aspiration of material into the lungs. S. aureus pneumonia is most often seen in IV drug users, patients who are immunocompromised, or as a complication of influenza infection. A history of both alcoholism and diabetes increases risk of pneumonia from K. pneumoniae, S. pneumoniae, P. aeruginosa, and H. influenzae.

stem: You are caring for a patient with hypertension and administer Labetalol. Given that you know Labetalol is a beta blocker, what class of antidysrhythmics are beta blockers?
rationale: Class II antidysrhythmics are beta blockers. These medications reduce heart rate and, thereby, are one means to lower a patient's blood pressure. An easy way to remember beta blockers are class II is that "B" is the second letter in the alphabet.

stem: A 24-year old military recruit on a remote training mission complained to his platoon medic of feeling quite ill with a rapid onset headache, dizziness, nausea, and feeling very weak. Exam was notable for a temperature of 103.5F, HR of 118, with neck stiffness, nausea, and no rash. The medevac is called, and you are transporting him to the nearest emergency department. What is the empiric treatment for the suspected condition?
options: Cefotaxime 2 grams IV and Ceftriaxone 2 grams IV
Doxycycline 200 mg PO and Clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h
Ceftriaxone 2 grams IV and Vancomycin 15 to 20 milligrams/kg IV
answer: Cefotaxime 2 grams IV and Ceftriaxone 2 grams IV
rationale: Bacterial meningitis is a life-threatening emergency that affects about 1.2 millions individuals worldwide each year. The presentation of fever, headache, stiff neck, and altered mental status is commonly seen in patients with bacterial meningitis. Most patients have at least two of four of these symptoms, but their absence does not exclude meningitis. Headache is the most common symptom and is seen in more than 85% of patients. Fever is the second most common symptom. Seizures and focal neurologic deficits are seen in 25% to 30% of patients. Evaluate for focal neurologic dysfunction such as hemiparesis, visual field deficits, or disordered eye movements. Increased intracranial pressure can cause papilledema or cranial nerve palsy especially involving cranial nerves III-VII. Assess for meningeal irritation with gentle neck flexion and extension, or Brudzinski sign (flexion of hips and knees in response to passive neck flexion) and Kernig sign (contraction of the hamstrings in response to knee extension while the hip is flexed). Examine the skin for cutaneous stigmata such as petechiae, splinter hemorrhages, and pustules. Percuss the sinuses and examine the ears for signs of primary infection. After addressing airway, breathing, and circulation status, immediately initiate empiric antibiotic therapy if bacterial meningitis is clinically suspected. Never delay administration of empiric antibiotic therapy for neuroimaging or to perform LP, because antibiotic treatment takes precedence over definitive diagnosis. The empiric antibiotic regimen for adults between 18 and 49 years of age is a third-generation cephalosporin, such as cefotaxime 2 grams IV or ceftriaxone 2 grams IV, plus vancomycin 15 to 20 milligrams/kg IV to achieve serum trough concentrations of 15 to 20 micrograms/mL, to cover the common pathogens S. pneumoniae and N. meningitidis.

stem: You are the CFRN called to a scene of a 14-year-old girl who was found unresponsive. First responders report that the mother called EMS when she found her daughter on the bedroom floor and could not wake her up. Patient's mother found what appears to be a suicide note on her daughter's bed. An empty pill bottle was found near the patient's bed. You place the patient on a 12 lead EKG and find a sinus tachycardia with a widened QRS and a prolonged QT interval. Which of the following drugs did the patient most likely take?
rationale: Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) are drugs that block sodium channels. An overdose of this type of drug causes the QRS and QT interval to be extremely widened and prolonged. All other drugs listed do not produce the mentioned EKG changes.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a 60-year-old female. The patient is bradycardic on the heart monitor. The patient is complaining of fatigue, blurred vision, and seeing a yellow-green halo in her vision. What medication is the patient most likely taking?
rationale: Digoxin overdose may be acute, chronic, intentional, or unintentional; it often presents with GI, constitutional, and/or cardiovascular symptoms. At therapeutic digoxin doses (0.5 to 0.9 nanograms/mL), ECG often shows PR prolongation and a scooped ST segment. In overdose, ECG frequently shows premature ventricular contractions, atrioventricular nodal blockade, and or slowed ventricular response. Treatment includes digoxin-specific antibody fragments and supportive care. Although not as common in digoxin overdose as it is in digitoxin overdose, visual disturbances of color vision with a tendency to perceive yellow halos around objects (xanthopsia), blurred vision, and diplopia may also be observed in digoxin overdose. The most likely signs and symptoms to be expected with amiloride overdose are dehydration and electrolyte imbalances. Signs/symptoms of amitriptyline overdose include abnormally low blood pressure, confusion, convulsions, dilated pupils and other eye problems, disturbed concentration, drowsiness, hallucinations, impaired heart function, rapid or irregular heartbeat, reduced body temperature, stupor, and unresponsiveness or coma. Imipramine overdose information is limited, but likely to include tremor, convulsions, seizure, tachycardia, somnolence, stupor.

stem: You are providing the initial medical air transport for a patient from the field and determine the patient has experienced an acute coronary syndrome (ACS) event. All of the following should be administered in initial immediate treatment of this patient, except:
rationale: Immediate, initial treatment of a patient who is experiencing ACS includes the administration of oxygen, nitroglycerin, aspirin, and morphine, according to current ACLS guidelines. Aspirin is administered to inhibit thromboxane, a stimulator of platelet aggregation, in order to prevent the worsening of any clot formation as a result of atherosclerotic plaque rupture. Nitroglycerin serves to relax vascular smooth muscle, increasing systemic vasodilation, resulting in a decrease in preload and the tension of the ventricles, as well as an overall decrease in blood pressure. Morphine is administered to both treat the pain associated with ACS events, as well as to relieve increases in cardiac workload that would be caused by stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system as a result of the pain.

stem: Nitroglycerin (NTG) should be used with extreme caution when treating which of the following patients?
options: Patient has ST elevation in V2-V4
Patient has a bundle branch block
Patient's EKG shows ST elevation in II, III, and AVF
answer: Patient's EKG shows ST elevation in II, III, and AVF
rationale: Patients with ST elevation in the inferior leads II, III, and AVF are at risk for right ventricular infarction as well. These patients are preload dependent. NTG causes vasodilation and, therefore, venous pooling in the vena cava, thereby reducing blood flow to the right ventricle which could cause further cardiac compromise. A bundle branch block is not a contraindication by itself to giving nitroglycerin if that patient is experiencing chest pain. A patient with anteroseptal ST elevation would benefit from the administration of NTG. A patient with high blood pressure would benefit from the use of NTG if they were experiencing chest pain.

stem: You are providing care for a patient in septic shock. Each of the following are considered standard components of care in patients experiencing septic shock, except:
options: Administer vasopressors to maintain MAP of ≥ 65 mm Hg
rationale: The standard components of care for patients experiencing septic shock include the administration of intravenous fluids to establish euvolemia, the administration of vasopressor medications to obtain and maintain a MAP (mean arterial pressure) of ≥ 65 mm Hg, and the administration of the most appropriate antimicrobial agent to treat the infectious process which contributed to the development of sepsis. Norepinephrine is most frequently used to maintain the MAP; if the administration of one vasopressor agent does not adequately support MAP, the addition of a second-line vasopressor, such as vasopressin, may be warranted. In patients who require the administration of two vasopressor agents, the additional administration of a mineralocorticoid steroid is recommended and has been found to be beneficial.

stem: Which of the following types of intracranial bleeding is most likely to be associated with the development of hydrocephalus?
rationale: Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH), which may result from traumatic injury to the head or spontaneously (often due to the unknown presence of an aneurysm), often presents with a starfish-like pattern on computed tomography (CT) scan. The blood which collects between the arachnoid membrane and the surface of the brain (pia mater) may extend into the intraventricular system due to the communication between these areas. Hydrocephalus may occur due to blockage of the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) flow which results from the SAH, worsening, or causing, increased intracranial pressure (ICP). The hydrocephalus is typically treated with administration of acetazolamide and mannitol, as well as surgical placement of a ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt.

stem: If a patient has second-degree frostbite, which of the following is affected?
options: The epidermis, the dermis, the subcutaneous tissue, the muscle, the tendons, and potentially the bone
The epidermis, the dermis, and the subcutaneous tissue
rationale: Frostbite results from the cooling of the body tissue to the point of ice crystal formation and most often involves the distal extremities. Although it is commonly associated with below-freezing temperatures, it can be produced at above-freezing temperatures by wind, altitude, humidity, and prolonged exposure. Frostbite is classified as a first-, second-, or third-degree injury. First-degree injury: superficial freezing without blistering or peeling is characterized by hyperemia and edema and involves the epidermis only. The tissue becomes mottled, cyanotic, and painful after rewarming. Second-degree injury: produces blistering or peeling of the skin and involves the epidermis and dermis; it is characterized by hyperemia and vesicle or bleb formation. When rewarmed, the skin is deep red, hot, and dry to the touch. Third-degree injury: involves the death of the dermis and even deeper tissue such as muscle and bone. Prehospital care focuses on protecting the affected area from trauma and partial thawing.

stem: During transport of a patient with symptomatic bradycardia, refractory to atropine, an IV infusion of epinephrine at a rate of 2 mcg/min is determined appropriate. Your supplies include 1 mL vials of 1:1000 epinephrine solution and 500 mL bags of normal saline. Which of the following is correct?
options: Mix 1 mL vial with 500 mL NS and infuse at 10 mL/h
Mix 1 mL vial with 500 mL NS and infuse at 6 mL/h
Mix 1 mL vial with 500 mL NS and infuse at 1 mL/h
Mix 1 mL vial with 500 mL NS and infuse at 60 mL/h
answer: Mix 1 mL vial with 500 mL NS and infuse at 60 mL/h
rationale: Bradycardia may be difficult to resolve until the underlying cause is treated. If atropine is ineffective, epinephrine infusion is a reasonable next step. An epinephrine vial (concentration of 1:1000) contains 1 mg/mL. To calculate the desired dose: 60 min/h x 2 mcg/min x 1 mg/1000 mcg x 500 mL/1 mg. Dose: 2 mcg/min. Time conversion: 60 min/h. Unit conversion: 1 mg/1000 mcg. Infusion concentration: 1 mg/500 mL. Therefore: 60 x 2 x (1/1000) x 500 = 60 mL/h.

stem: What would be the result of Beta 2 receptor stimulation?
rationale: We need to understand the effects different receptors have on the body. A good way to remember this is that you have one heart and two lungs and that Beta 1 works primarily on the heart while Beta 2 works in the lungs and blood vessels. Alpha 1 is a vasoconstrictor and a decrease in heart rate would be a cholinergic response.

stem: The medical air transport crew is transporting a patient who has hyponatremia of uncertain origin, and calculation of the patient's serum osmolality reveals an osmolality that is within normal range for the adult patient. All of the following findings cause pseudohyponatremia, except:
rationale: Serum osmolality is calculated through the use of a simplified, standardized formula using some of the patient's chemistry laboratory values (sodium, BUN, and glucose), and is typically used in instances when the serum sodium level is low and of uncertain origin. Serum osmolality is also often used in calculating the osmolar gap, which can then be used to narrow the gap of the differential diagnosis list, and typically indicates the presence of an additional solute in the patient's serum (often an alcohol). A finding of hyponatremia associated with a normal serum osmolality is referred to as pseudohyponatremia, and is found in patients with gross elevations of serum glucose, grossly elevated lipid levels, and in conditions where there is a gross elevation of protein, such as in multiple myeloma or after receiving infusion of immunoglobulins.

stem: You are caring for a patient who has received a heart transplant. The patient begins to show signs of shock, is hypotensive and bradycardic with a pulse on the monitor. Which intervention is most appropriate?
rationale: Patients who have received a heart transplant will respond best to external pacing for bradycardia. Atropine does not work in heart transplant patients because the vagus nerve to the heart has been severed during surgery. Synchronized cardioversion is used to treat SVT. The patient still has a pulse at this point, therefore, chest compressions are not needed.

stem: All of the following may contribute to the development of hyponatremia except:
rationale: The development of hyponatremia may result in abnormalities of cardiac conduction, behavior problems, and neurologic problems such as headache, disorientation, tremors, weakness, and muscle twitching. Seizures, psychosis, and coma may result from sodium levels below 110 mEq/L. Hyponatremia may develop as a result of liver disease, congestive heart failure, kidney failure or kidney lesions, as a result of heavy exercise, dehydration, excessive water ingestion, gastrointestinal losses such as vomiting or diarrhea, and from syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH). Ingestion of sodium bicarbonate antacids may cause the development of hypernatremia.

stem: A patient presenting with muffled heart tones, narrowed pulse pressures, jugular vein distention, and pulsus alternans on EKG would benefit from which procedure?
rationale: Patients experiencing these signs, also known as Beck's triad, are suffering from cardiac tamponade. The treatment of cardiac tamponade is pericardiocentesis, where a needle is inserted into the pericardium, the sac that surrounds the heart, and draining off excess fluid. This fluid is usually blood in the case of trauma patients. Needle decompression and chest tube placement is used to treat pneumothorax. The patient is not suffering from a disease process in which intubation is the definitive treatment.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient in septic shock. Which of the following should you administer first?
rationale: Patients with septic shock can be identified with a clinical construct of sepsis with persisting hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain a mean MAP ≥65 mm Hg and having a serum lactate level >2 mmol/L despite adequate volume resuscitation. For the management of septic shock, a protocol with a goal of effective resuscitation in the first 6 hours is well known to improve results. Circulation and perfusion are addressed first, so IV fluids should be started immediately (volume resuscitation to euvolemia). Early support of the MAP to at least 65 mm Hg using vasopressors if needed is the next step, followed by early appropriate antimicrobial therapy. Although the role of steroids is unclear, the administration of a single dose of a mineralocorticoid steroid for septic shock patients who require a second vasopressor for BP support is a reasonable intervention.

stem: During medical air transport of a patient with duodenal ulceration, the patient becomes motion sick and begins vomiting. Within minutes, the patient begins vomiting bright red blood and experiences an acute hemorrhagic episode. The medical transport team should take all of the following steps in intervening for this patient, except:
options: Ensure the Linton tube is venting adequately
rationale: Any patient being transported by medical air transport is at risk for developing motion sickness. Patients who are diagnosed with peptic or duodenal ulcers are at risk of ulcer rupture as a result of forceful vomiting and should be medicated with antiemetics prior to takeoff of the transport mission to prevent this possibility. Any gastric tubes, including Linton tubes or Minnesota tubes, should be regularly assessed during the flight to ensure they are adequately vented. If ulcer rupture occurs, treatment focus should be on cessation of bleeding, replacing lost blood volume and maintenance of fluid volume status, providing adequate gastric venting, and maintaining the patient's airway. Epinephrine can be administered endoscopically to aid in cessation of bleeding, but focus during medical transport should be on preventing the development of hemorrhagic shock and treatment for volume loss, maintaining adequate systemic blood pressure through the use of vasopressors if needed, and maintaining adequate ventilation.

stem: Provision of emergency care to a patient experiencing ventricular fibrillation (V-fib) includes administration of any of the following medications, except:
rationale: In ventricular fibrillation (V-fib), one of the ventricular dysrhythmias, there are typically multiple ectopic intrinsic pacemakers within the ventricles, causing ventricular chaos as the ventricles attempt to contract in response to the multiple stimuli. As a result of this disorganization, no effective ventricular contraction occurs, resulting in a quivering, or fibrillation, of the ventricles which is incapable of sustaining life. The best treatment for V-fib is immediate defibrillation, delivered in an attempt to "shock" the cardiac muscle back into a state of synchronous contraction. When medications are indicated for treatment of V-fib, antiarrhythmic drugs, such as amiodarone and lidocaine, are typically used, as is epinephrine. Vasopressin, which had been previously recommended as appropriate replacement for the first or second doses of epinephrine typically administered during cardiac arrest resuscitation, is no longer included in the most recent ACLS pharmacologic recommendations as its use has not shown any advantages over that of epinephrine.

stem: Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) Syndrome presents as which of the following?
options: Regular R to R intervals and regular P to P intervals but no consistent PR intervals
"Slurred" R wave on EKG with runs of SVT
answer: "Slurred" R wave on EKG with runs of SVT
rationale: WPW syndrome is caused by an accessory pathway called the bundle of Kent. This shows on the EKG as a delta wave, which is described as a slurred R wave. WPW typically has a narrow QRS. A rhythm with no consistent PR intervals but consistent R to R and P to P intervals is a complete heart block. A prolonged PR interval is a first degree heart block.

stem: You are caring for a patient and notice ST segment elevation in leads II and III. What does this indicate?
rationale: Leads II, III, and AVF all look at the inferior surface of the heart.

stem: All of the following patients are most likely to sustain ventricular fibrillation as a result of electrical injury, except?
options: two-year-old who sucks on a low-tension electrical cord
16-year-old girl washing her hands in a sink when a blow dryer is dropped into the sink
42-year-old mechanic working on a car battery
35-year-old female who is struck by lightning while using her laptop inside her home
answer: 42-year-old mechanic working on a car battery
rationale: The term electrocution is used when describing the cause of death of an individual who came into contact with electrical energy, while the term electrical injury is used to describe the outcome of an individual who came into contact with electrical energy and survived. Injury sustained when an individual completes the circuit of electricity may be caused by high- or low-voltage (force) electricity and may occur through alternating current (AC) or direct current (DC) electricity. Most household appliances make use of low-voltage, AC current, while car batteries and battery chargers typically make use of DC electricity. Exposure to AC electricity causes tetanic muscle contractions which result in the injured patient being unable to let go of the electricity source, resulting in significant electrical burns. Individuals exposed to AC are most likely to experience ventricular fibrillation as a result of the tetanic chest muscle contractions that result from the AC. DC, in contrast, typically produces one single strong muscle contraction when the individual is exposed to the current; this often results in the electrically-injured individual being "thrown" away from the source of the electricity. Lightning strikes also often result in ventricular fibrillation, as well as other cardiac rhythm disturbances.

stem: You are providing care for a patient who has experienced an acute lower gastrointestinal (GI) bleed as a result of a strangulated bowel due to the presence of adhesions. Which of the following statements regarding laboratory findings in patients who have experienced lower GI bleeding is most accurate?
options: Creatinine level is often elevated in lower GI bleeding
Laboratory results are often normal for 48 hours following the lower GI bleeding
Neutropenia is often noted in laboratory findings of patients who have experienced lower GI bleeding
Hemoglobin level is typically below 10 gm/dL within 24 hours of the lower GI bleeding
answer: Laboratory results are often normal for 48 hours following the lower GI bleeding
rationale: Lower GI bleeding may occur as a result of diverticulitis, hernias, the presence of adhesions, as a result of inflammatory bowel disorders such as Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis, or the presence of foreign objects in the lower GI tract. Laboratory studies are often normal for the first 24 to 48 hours following an acute lower GI bleed, and changes after that time often include a decrease in hemoglobin (Hgb \< 10 requires transfusion) and hematocrit (due to interstitial fluid shifting), thrombocytopenia, and increases in white blood count (WBC) and blood urea nitrogen (BUN) levels.

stem: Which of the following laboratory studies is often referred to as the "poor man's ABG"?
rationale: Serum sodium, chloride, and bicarbonate levels are evaluated together to determine what is referred to as the anion gap. The anion gap represents the difference between the number of positively charged ions and negatively charged ions (cations and anions), and can indicate a state of acidosis in the event of a large gap. The formula for calculating the anion gap is as follows: Anion Gap = (Na+) − (Cl⁻ + HCO3⁻). A normal anion gap is considered to be 12 (+/−4). The acidosis is considered to be worse with greater anion gap (larger calculated number).

stem: Which of the following cardiac dysrhythmias which originates in the atrioventricular (AV) node could be most accurately described as being characterized by a progressive lengthening of the PR interval until ultimately a QRS complex is dropped?
rationale: Cardiac dysrhythmias may originate from multiple sites in the heart, including the sinoatrial (SA) node, the arterioventricular (AV) node, the atria, or the ventricles. First-, second-, and third-degree AV block dysrhythmias all result from a partial or complete disruption in the electrical impulse transmitted by the AV node. First-degree AV block is characterized by a constant prolonging of the PR interval (greater than 0.2 second) with identical P waves preceding each QRS complex. Second-degree AV blocks, of which there are two types, are also called Mobitz type blocks. Mobitz I, also referred to as Wenckebach AV block, is characterized by a progressive lengthening of the PR interval until ultimately a QRS complex is "dropped." This pattern is then repeated, repeatedly. Often, patients with Wenckebach AV block do not require treatment if they are asymptomatic. Mobitz type II AV block is characterized by a constant PR interval with identical P waves preceding QRS complexes (when present). There is no progressive prolongation of the PR interval in this second-degree block. Mobitz II is more serious than Mobitz I, and requires treatment, as it often develops into third-degree heart block. Third-degree AV block, also referred to as complete heart block, is often lethal. The action of the atria and ventricles are controlled by separate intrinsic pacemakers, with dissociation of the P waves from the QRS complex. Third-degree block must be treated immediately, ideally with pacing, or with pharmacologic methods (atropine, or dopamine or epinephrine).

stem: You are the CFRN called for an inter-facility transfer of a 50-year-old male diagnosed with an aortic dissection. Which of the following findings would you expect to see in a patient with aortic dissection?
options: difference of 20 mmHg systolic BP in each arm
answer: difference of 20 mmHg systolic BP in each arm
rationale: A difference in systolic BP is a common finding in patients with an aortic dissection, especially in the presence of a descending dissection. Pulsus alternans typically is seen with patients suffering cardiac tamponade on the EKG. Hemoptisis is not a sign of aortic dissection. Global ST elevation is seen in pericarditis.

stem: A patient is suffering from cyanide poisoning. As a CFRN, you know that cyanide prevents cellular uptake of oxygen. What kind of hypoxia is the patient most likely to suffer from?
rationale: Cyanide poisoning prevents cells from using oxygen, therefore, the hypoxia is histotoxic in nature. Hypemic hypoxia would result from loss of blood. Stagnant hypoxia would result from a massive pulmonary embolism or any condition where blood flow is interrupted. Hypoxic hypoxia would result from there being too little oxygen to breathe in.

stem: A 10-year-old boy experiencing respiratory distress is agitated and leaning forward over the kitchen table. Speaking in short phrases, he tells you that he has asthma but does not carry an inhaler. You observe nasal flaring, severe suprasternal intercostal retractions, and hear decreased air movement with prolonged expiratory time and wheezing. You provide 100% oxygen by non-rebreathing mask, and the boy's SpO2 is 90%. Which medication should you prepare to administer?
rationale: Physical signs are useful in determining the severity of airway obstruction, while mental status changes (i.e., agitation) may indicate hypoxemia; hypercarbia may be indicated by increased somnolence. Determine oxygen saturation at presentation. Perform continuous pulse oximetry for patients who are hypoxemic or severely ill. Provide supplemental oxygen for oxygen saturation \< 92%, and maintain at 93% to 98% because very high saturations of oxygen may decrease minute ventilation and result in elevations of pulmonary end tidal carbon dioxide (ETCO2). Oxygen and beta-2 agonists are pulmonary vasodilators and may contribute to ventilation-perfusion mismatch by augmenting perfusion of underventilated lung units. This phenomenon may worsen hypoxemia, but generally resolves by 1 to 2 hours as pulmonary autoregulation corrects the mismatch. If oxygen saturation remains \< 92% after initial treatment, consider worsening asthma, pneumothorax, or pneumonia.

stem: A patient with Marfan's syndrome presents with sudden onset pain in the interscapular region that radiates into his legs. Patient has a history of hypertension and is non-compliant with his medications. Patient's blood pressure is 185/95. You recheck the blood pressure in the other arm and find a blood pressure of 160/93. What is the probable cause of the patient's symptoms?
rationale: Marfan's syndrome is a disorder of the connective tissue where connective tissue degenerates. A common place for this to occur is the elastic fibers of the aortic media. This breakdown allows for a dissection to occur. This information, along with history of hypertension and differing blood pressures in each arm, leads us to believe aortic dissection is the cause of the patient's symptoms.

stem: The medical transport team is providing air transport for a patient who has unintentionally overdosed on his medication. The patient is reporting green and yellow visual halos and has flu-like symptoms. Blood work reveals hyperkalemia, and electrocardiogram findings include normal sinus rhythm with a slurred upslope of the QRS complex. Based on these findings, which of the following cardiac medications did this patient most likely ingest?
rationale: The cardiac glycoside drugs have been in use since at least the 1500s both to treat cardiac issues and as a poison which was used against one's enemies. Digitalis is the most well-known of the cardiac glycosides, and is derived from the foxglove plant. Therapeutically, it is used in the treatment of both congestive heart failure and cardiac arrhythmias, and increases cardiac contractility. Due to its narrow therapeutic window, even small dosing errors of digoxin can produce toxicity. Patients who experience inadvertent digitalis poisoning may experience both cardiac and non-cardiac symptoms, including flu-like symptoms, the presence of green or yellow visual halos, hallucinations, true psychosis, restlessness or confusion, photophobia, and nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Electrocardiogram findings may be normal and may reveal a slurring of the QRS complex upslope, or the patient may develop significant bradydysrhythmias originating through the sinoatrial or atrioventricular nodes. Hyperkalemia frequently results from digoxin toxicity.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a post CABG patient who has an intra aortic balloon pump (IABP) placed to reduce the workload on their heart. Prior to patient transport, the nurse at the sending facility reported the patient had a normal urine output of 40 mL/hr. In the hour-long transport to the receiving facility, you notice that the patient has decreased urine output. What is the most likely cause?
options: The patient's bypass graft has ruptured
The patient is in septic shock resulting from the surgery
The helium in the tank has ran out, causing the balloon to not inflate
The balloon is occluding the renal arteries
answer: The balloon is occluding the renal arteries
rationale: The correct position for the IABP to rest is distal to the left subclavian artery and above the renal arteries. If the patient is experiencing oliguria or anuria, the IABP can be occluding the renal arteries causing reduced blood flow to the kidneys, thereby decreasing urine production.

stem: Your patient has sinus bradycardia (36 bpm), received 3 mg atropine, and a failed attempt at transcutaneous pacing. What is the next best step?
rationale: For adult sinus bradycardia (HR \< 60 bpm), refractory to atropine and attempted transcutaneous pacing, the 2020 AHA ACLS algorithm suggests administration of an IV infusion of either epinephrine (2--10 mcg/minute) or dopamine (5--20 mcg/kg per minute), titrated to patient response. Failing these measures, expert consultation is recommended with the possible attempt at transvenous pacing.

stem: While transporting a patient with an IABP, the IABP experiences a power failure. Which of the following interventions is correct?
options: Contact medical direction to discuss removal of the IABP as soon as possible
Attach 60-mL syringe to the proximal stopcock and inflate the IABP catheter once every 3--5 minutes to prevent clot formation on the catheter
answer: Attach 60-mL syringe to the proximal stopcock and inflate the IABP catheter once every 3--5 minutes to prevent clot formation on the catheter
rationale: For a patient with an IABP who goes into cardiopulmonary arrest/asystole or pulseless electrical activity, the flight crew should initiate ACLS guidelines, and change the IABP to pressure mode during compression. For a patient with an IABP experiencing ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia arrest, initiate ACLS guidelines, and leave the IABP in current settings. The pump is grounded and can accommodate electrical shocks. If diastolic hypertension is detected, provide afterload reduction or decrease augmentation volume in the IABP if possible. If the patient develops a rapid heart rate > 200, leave the IABP in 1:1 counterpulsation. In the event of a balloon rupture (blood in the sheath, loss of gas alarm, and rust-colored flecks), contact medical direction, as removal of the IABP as soon as possible is needed. Power failure of an IABP alone is not an indication for administration of epinephrine.

stem: A patient presents with difficulty breathing and coughing up pink frothy sputum. The patient's chest X-ray shows an enlarged heart that is more than half the width of the chest. The patient's lab values show an elevated BNP of 800 pg/mL. Which condition is the patient most likely suffering from?
rationale: The above signs and symptoms are typical of patients suffering from congestive heart failure. In this condition, the heart has been weakened and is ineffective as a pump. As such, the heart and pulmonary circulation begin to back up with fluid. As the heart has to pump harder and harder, it becomes larger and less efficient. A chest X-ray with a cardiac shadow greater than one-half of the chest cavity is a common sign. BNP is released from the ventricles when they are overstretched. Increased levels show the extent of heart failure. Any value of more than 100 pg/mL indicates some level of heart failure, with severe heart failure occurring at over 900 pg/mL. The progressing cough and frothy sputum are indicative of pulmonary edema caused by a backup of fluid into the lungs due to left sided heart failure. Left sided heart failure will eventually cause right sided heart failure. Aortic dissection would present with a patient having extreme back pain, diaphoresis, and a difference in blood pressures in each arm. A pulmonary embolism would not produce frothy sputum.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient who called EMS complaining of sudden onset of chest pain. Patient's EKG showed ST elevation in V2--V6. Initially, patient was alert and oriented but during transport, the patient begins to show signs of altered mental status. Patient's skin is cool and clammy. Patient's blood pressure is 80/40 and pulses are thready. Which condition is the patient most likely experiencing?
rationale: Given that the patient has ST elevation in leads V2--V6, we know this involves the left ventricle. The patient begins to show signs of poor tissue perfusion (shock) with altered mental status and cool, clammy skin. Ventricular damage as a result of infarct not treated in a timely manner will lead to heart failure and, eventually, cardiogenic shock.

stem: Which of the following electrolyte abnormalities is considered to be the most dangerous?
rationale: Potassium is the primary cation in intracellular function and, as such, abnormalities in potassium level pose the greatest danger to health. Abnormalities in potassium level frequently causes electrocardiogram (ECG) changes, although these changes do not correspond with severity of the imbalance. Hyperkalemia will cause the development of tall, peaked T-waves on ECG, with associated flattening of the P wave and widening of the QRS complex. Patients may be bradycardic. Hypokalemia causes depression or flattening of the T-waves on ECG. Both conditions require urgent (or emergent) treatment.

stem: The medical air transport team has been called to transport a 24-year-old female who has experienced a medication overdose. Which of the following is the most commonly ingested drug that results in drug overdose?
rationale: Acetaminophen is the most commonly ingested drug which results in drug overdose; it is contained in combination formulas of many over-the-counter (OTC) medications in addition to being available in single formulation. Individuals may experience inadvertent or accidental overdose of acetaminophen as a result of being unaware of its presence in these combination formulas when they attempt to treat symptoms through the use of several OTC medications. Acetaminophen is absorbed within the small intestine; however, toxicity results from the presence of metabolites that attach to the hepatic cell membrane. Glutathione, an antioxidant stored in the liver, neutralizes the effects of the acetaminophen metabolites, but in the instance of overdose, depletion of glutathione stores results and hepatic damage occurs.

stem: When is the use of magnesium indicated during cardiac arrest?
options: Pulseless ventricular tachycardia-associated torsades de pointes
Ventricular tachycardia with a normal QT interval
answer: Pulseless ventricular tachycardia-associated torsades de pointes
rationale: The management of ventricular tachycardia is based on the patient's clinical stability. The most extreme form of instability is cardiac arrest; these patients should receive prompt electrical defibrillation, chest compressions, and other advanced life support measures. Patients with a pulse, but compromised by the ventricular tachycardia, should undergo electrical cardioversion, coupled with administration of a procedural analgesia and sedation if clinical status allows. In the stable patient, pharmacologic agents are first-line therapy. Hemodynamically stable patients with ventricular tachycardia are treated with procainamide, amiodarone, lidocaine, or magnesium. Magnesium is primarily used as an antiarrhythmic agent in patients with known hypomagnesemia, QT interval prolongation, polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, or torsades de pointes. Definitive treatment of torsades de pointes is ventricular pacing at a rate necessary to prevent the bursts of polymorphous ventricular tachycardia. Pacing is continued until the cause of QT prolongation (drug toxicity, electrolyte abnormality) is ameliorated.

stem: During transport of a 34-year-old female patient, ECG indicates a narrow complex tachycardia. She has not respond to two delivered doses of adenosine (6 mg, and 12 mg IV push). She is sedated and cardioversion is attempted, after which, she develops a wide-complex polymorphic tachycardia with palpable peripheral pulses. What likely caused this change in cardiac rhythm?
rationale: Electrical energy can terminate an abnormal rhythm but, if inappropriately delivered, it may also induce ventricular fibrillation. This can happen if the electric shock is delivered during the relative refractory period of the cardiac electrical cycle. Movement artifacts or loose leads may resemble ventricular fibrillation and result in an inappropriate shock. Synchronizing the defibrillator ensures that the shock is delivered at the QRS complex. If the shock is delivered during the T wide-complex, it risks developments of a wide-complex polymorphic tachycardia, also known as "torsades des points." The 2020 ACLS updated guidelines include emphasis on high-performance resuscitation teams. Some strategies adopted by these teams include the practice of precharging a defibrillator 15 seconds before the 2-minute cycle or providing chest compressions while charging and resuming chest compression immediately after a shock. Another recommended strategy is that the second rescuer hover their hands over the patient, ready to immediately recommence after defibrillation.

stem: A patient you are caring for is complaining of headache, has sensitivity to light, and nuchal rigidity. When you perform an assessment, the patient has Kernig's and Brudzinski's sign. What condition does the patient most likely have?
rationale: Meningitis typically presents with the acute onset of the triad of headache, fever, and a stiff neck, and may occur in the setting of a cluster of cases. Kernig's sign is an inability to straighten the leg when the hip is flexed due to stiff hamstrings; Brudzinski's sign is the appearance of involuntary lifting of the legs when lifting a patient's head. These are present in about 60% of patients with meningitis. The presentation of fever and headache is common and can be worrisome, potentially caused by anything from influenza to meningitis, but when coupled with nuchal rigidity or altered mental status should generate a strong suspicion for meningitis, a potentially life-threatening illness, with a mortality rate of about 25%. Patients with suppressed immune systems and the elderly are less likely to have a stiff neck. Lumbar puncture is the only means of making a definitive diagnosis. Because of the severity of meningeal infections, empiric therapy is recommended while waiting for Gram stain and culture results. Antibiotic treatment should be ordered when the diagnosis of meningitis is suspected and given immediately after CSF begins to be collected.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient with an electrical injury. Which of the following potentially lethal cardiac dysrhythmias is most often caused by electrical current passing through the body?
rationale: As electrical current passes through the body, severe dysrhythmia may occur. Ventricular fibrillation, which is fatal, is frequently induced as a 60-cycle alternating current passes through the ventricles. In addition to this fatal rhythm, other dysrhythmias may occur, such as atrial fibrillation, sinus bradycardia, ventricular and atrial ectopy, supraventricular tachycardia, bundle branch block, and first- and second-degree block.

stem: A patient in renal failure secondary to trauma from a motor vehicle accident (MVA) is most likely to suffer from which of the following hematological problems long-term?
rationale: The kidney is responsible for the production of erythropoietin, which acts on the bone marrow to increase the production of red blood cells (RBCs). Iron, folic acid, and vitamin B12 are also needed for RBC production. A patient in renal failure is most likely to suffer from anemia because of the reduced production of the hormone erythropoietin.

stem: What intervention is indicated for a patient experiencing hypotension, jugular vein distinction, muffled heart tones, electrical alternans on the EKG, and narrowing pulse pressures?
options: Obtain IV access and start levophed
Needle decompression and definitive chest tube placement
Bilateral IVs and fluid bonus; if hypotension does not resolve, perform pericardiocentesis
Positive pressure ventilations, prepare for RSI
answer: Bilateral IVs and fluid bonus; if hypotension does not resolve, perform pericardiocentesis
rationale: The patient is experiencing cardiac tamponade. The listed signs and symptoms are Beck's triad and EKG changes associated with tamponade. Fluid needs to be removed from the pericardial sac in order to increase cardiac output. Needle decompression and chest tube placement would be the treatment for a tension pneumothorax. Positive pressure ventilations and RSI would be for someone unable to manage their own airway/unable to oxygenate or ventilate. Starting levophed would cause too much systemic vascular resistance that the heart could not overcome.

stem: You observe changes in consciousness in your patient who has sustained a cerebral vascular accident (CVA). Which position would be most beneficial for this patient during transport?
options: Head of the bed elevated to 90°
Prone with the head turned to the right
Flat with the head turned to the left
rationale: In the setting of suspected elevated ICP, elevation of the head of the bed to 30° is currently recommended as a simple maneuver to improve cerebral blood flow; in addition, the patient's head should be kept at midline to aid in venous drainage. Elevation of 30 degrees can reduce the mean pressure within the brain by 10--15 mm Hg and improve CPP. CPP = MAP − ICP Note that lowering the ICP will improve CPP, but lowering MAP, especially in the setting of hypotension, could be counterproductive and lower CPP. Mannitol (0.5 to 1.0 gram/kg IV) can decrease intravascular volume and brain water and may transiently reduce ICP. Hypertonic saline infusion has also been found to be effective in reducing ICP. In cases of brain edema associated with tumor, dexamethasone, 10 milligrams IV, reduces edema over several hours. Hyperventilation with reduction of partial pressure of arterial carbon dioxide can reduce cerebral blood volume and transiently lower ICP. Current recommendations are to avoid excessive hyperventilation (partial pressure of arterial carbon dioxide ≤ 35 mm Hg) during the first 24 hours after brain injury.

stem: A patient with which of the following would be at most risk for sepsis?
rationale: Bacteria will enter the peritoneal cavity with a colon rupture and can cause sepsis.

stem: Beck's Triad is seen in which of the following conditions?
rationale: Beck's Triad consists of jugular venous distention (JVD), muffled heart tones, and narrowed pulse pressures. As fluid accumulates around the heart, the preload is reduced, causing decreased cardiac output. This causes a backup of blood into the venous system and is seen as JVD. Sound is dampened when traveling through a liquid medium and therefore, the sound of the closure of AV, aortic, and pulmonic valves is harder to auscultate. Pulmonary embolism would not produce muffled heart sounds. Hypovolemic shock would not show JVD because of low blood volume. Increased intracranial pressure would not show any of the signs of Beck's Triad.

stem: All of the following are potential causes of obstructive shock (any age population), except:
rationale: Obstructive shock is defined as the presence of some type of obstruction which impairs cardiovascular flow, resulting in impaired filling of the ventricles during diastole, or an obstruction which impairs cardiovascular flow contributing to an increased afterload. Obstructive shock is most commonly caused by tension pneumothorax, pericardial tamponade, pulmonary embolism, or in pediatric patients, cardiac defects including aortic stenosis, interruptions to the aortic arch, and hypoplastic left heart.

stem: At what level should the ICP transducer be placed?
rationale: The interventricular foramina, also known as foramen of Monro, are part of the cerebral ventricular system and serve as a connection between the third and lateral ventricles. ICP is the most commonly monitored parameter in neurosurgical intensive care practice. The ICP value is important in itself, and also for calculating CPP values. Absolute values are dependent on the chosen reference point on which the gauge is placed. As such, the pressure transducer needs to be placed on the surface of the head in a position that relates to an internal structure which reflects accurate ICP. The Neuroanaesthesia Society of Great Britain and Ireland (NASGBI) and Society of British Neurological Surgeons (SBNS) recommend zeroing the arterial transducer at the level of the tragus, as do many US hospital and transport systems. Whatever reference point is used, the level of an external transducer needs to be adjusted with any change in head position.

stem: The best indicator of changes in intracranial pressure during transport of a patient is:
rationale: The best indicator of changes in intracranial pressure (ICP), especially from a mass lesion, is a patient's level of consciousness. The Glasgow Coma Scale remains the most appropriate method to describe and communicate the condition of an individual patient's level of consciousness by separate, multidimensional ratings of their eye, verbal, and motor responses. The Glasgow Coma Scale is comprised of the individual components, E(4) V(5) M(6). While motor response and eye opening are components, it is the relative overall measure of consciousness that may indicate a change in ICP.

stem: The components of the Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale include which of the following?
options: Facial smile/grimace, hand grip, and arm weakness assessment
Glasgow Coma Scale and leg and arm assessment
Level of consciousness and leg and arm assessment
Facial droop, arm drift, and speech assessment
answer: Facial droop, arm drift, and speech assessment
rationale: Three of the more commonly used prehospital tools are the Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale, the Los Angeles Prehospital Stroke Screen, and the Melbourne Ambulance Stroke Screen. The Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale assesses facial droop, arm drift, and speech. The Los Angeles Prehospital Stroke Screen assesses age, history of seizure disorder, new onset neurologic symptoms, ambulatory baseline, blood glucose level, asymmetry of the face (smile/grimace), grip, and arm strength. The Melbourne Ambulance Stroke Screen assesses age, history of seizure disorder, ambulatory status (i.e., wheelchair bound), blood glucose, facial droop, hand grip strength, arm drift, and speech.

stem: A 21-year-old female who presented with fever, a diffuse red rash, and hypotension is being transported. The patient's boyfriend states they returned earlier today from a camping trip in the mountains due to his girlfriend had reported feeling unwell. He states she is currently taking oral contraceptives, took acetaminophen earlier today for flu-like symptoms, and experienced a spontaneous abortion eight days ago at approximately six weeks of pregnancy (she did not seek treatment), and that they have been engaging in unprotected intercourse. Based on the information provided in the scenario, the patient's symptoms are most likely resulting from:
options: Strep throat complicated by the development of scarlet fever
rationale: Toxic shock syndrome (TSS) is a rare but potentially deadly complication of infection with either S. aureus or S. pyogenes. It occurs most frequently in menstruating women who use long-absorbing tampons, but also may occur in women with septic abortion, or in males (25% of cases). Septic abortion most frequently results after therapeutic abortion (TAB) was completed by an unlicensed or illegal abortion provider, but also may result after spontaneous abortion (SAB) (miscarriage). TSS presents with symptoms of myalgias, fever, diffuse red rash which is often reported as being sunburn-like, and hypotension. If not rapidly treated, septic shock may develop. Patients who present with symptoms of TSS should also be evaluated for other disease processes which cause diffuse red rash, including Rocky Mountain spotted fever, scarlet fever, and measles.

stem: While evaluating a critically ill patient you are transporting by medical aircraft, you note the presence of Trousseau sign. Which of the following abnormalities causes a positive Trousseau sign?
rationale: Hypocalcemia, which is indicated by a serum calcium level of less than 8.5 mEq/L, may be caused by a number of factors, including: acute pancreatitis, chronic renal failure, osteomalacia, hypoparathyroidism, malabsorption syndromes, or vitamin deficiency. The presence of hypocalcemia can be assessed for through elicitation of either Trousseau sign or Chvostek sign. To elicit Trousseau sign, a blood pressure cuff is applied and when inflated, tetany of the forearm muscles ensues. Chvostek sign is elicited by tapping on the facial nerve (immediately in front of the ear) and watching for the development of facial spasms. CVA may result in the development of decorticate posturing. Meningitis may result in the development of decerebrate posturing. Hypomagnesemia may co-exist with hypocalcemia and should be assessed for through the obtaining of laboratory studies.

stem: You are transporting a patient with severe hyperkalemia by medical air transport. All of the following are considered first-line treatments in the management of a patient with hyperkalemia except:
rationale: The development of hyperkalemia is one of the most dangerous electrolyte disturbances that can be experienced; hypokalemia is equally dangerous. Hyperkalemia can cause cardiac conduction abnormalities; electrocardiogram (ECG) changes resulting from hyperkalemia include a prolonged PR interval and widening of the QRS complex, as well as peaking of T-waves. First-line treatment for hyperkalemia includes affording cardioprotection through the administration of calcium gluconate (to prevent ventricular tachycardia), as well as methods to reduce serum potassium. First-line medications may be used to 'push' potassium back into the cells, such as bicarbonate, insulin, 50% dextrose, or albuterol. Second-line medications, such as furosemide and Kayexalate, are used to aid in excreting potassium through the urine or stool; dialysis is also considered a second-line treatment method for reducing hyperkalemia.
